summary: Jeff Atwood explores the limitations of regular expressions when parsing complex code structures. While acknowledging his love for regex and their effectiveness for common parsing tasks, he demonstrates how edge cases like nested tags and escaped characters can overwhelm even clever regex solutions. The post highlights Drazen Dotlic's work using the CoCo-R parser to colorize C# code, illustrating how full-blown parsers understand code in ways regex cannot. Atwood concludes that developers must sometimes abandon regex complexity and embrace proper parsing tools for challenging problems.
key_points: Regular expressions excel at solving day-to-day parsing problems cost-effectively, but full programming languages cannot be parsed with regex alone
Edge cases like nested HTML tags, escaped quotes, and improperly escaped characters expose fundamental regex limitations
Increasing regex complexity to handle edge cases eventually paints you into a corner
Parsers truly understand code structure while regex only matches patterns
CoCo-R and similar compiler-compiler tools provide a path forward when regex isn't enough
money_quotes: While you can hack around these problems with more and more regular expression cleverness, you eventually paint yourself into a corner with complexity.
Regular expressions don't truly understand the code that they are colorizing – but parsers do.
Depending on the problem you're attacking, at some point you have to bite the bullet and utilize a full-blown parser.
A full-blown programming language cannot be parsed with regular expressions.
There is an immensely bigger amount of common problems and small parsing needs that are very cost-effectively solved with regular expressions.
key_insight: Know when to abandon increasingly clever regex solutions and embrace proper parsing tools – regex matches patterns, but parsers understand structure.
